coredata.dc:title: Preparation of concave magnetoplasmonic core-shell supraparticles of gold-coated iron oxide via ion-reducible layer-by-layer method for surface enhanced Raman scattering
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Dong Kyu
Song, Younseong
Tran, Van Tan
Kim, Jeonghyo
Park, Enoch Y.
Lee, Jaebeom
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of suprastructure assemblies with unique colloidal and optical properties remains challenging. Non-uniform covering of magnetic nanoparticles (NPs) with an external inert Au shell has been attempted to protect the magnetic core against oxidation as well as to produce multifunctional supraparticles (SPs) possessing respective optical and magnetic properties. In this study, a concave Au NP coating was deposited on magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) with precise control of the shell thickness and roughness through a layer-by-layer (LbL) assisted ionic reduction method termed ion-reducible LbL (IR-LbL) method. Surface enhanced Raman spectra were obtained using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the magnetically aligned structure of the prepared core-shell SPs. It is probable that this synthesis method and the generated SPs are essential for characterizing the merge of electronics and magnetism in the nano-regime and may be applicable for further electronics, magnetic storage, and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Suprastructure
Supraparticles
Layer-by-layer
Core-shell
Magnetoplasmonic
Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Step I: synthesis of PEI-coated MNPs (MNP@PEI)
Step II: AuNP decorated MNPs (MNP@PEI@Au SPs) by LbL method
Step III: precise control of the shell thickness by IR-LbL process
Raman spectroscopy of 1 – dimensionally aligned MNP@Au SPs
Computational and experimental data of SERS on 1D-assembled concave MNP@Au SPs
SERS effect on rhodamine B and GQDs on magnetically self-assembled concave MNP@Au SPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation of supraparticles (SPs) by ion-reducible layer-by-layer (IR-LbL) method. (A) Synthesized magnetic NPs (size ∼100nm) were coated with polyethyleneimine (PEI); (B) Au NPs (size: 4nm) were coated on the PEI layer. (C) LbL process was repeated to grow Au layer and (D) final structure of SPs and schematic drawing for surface enhanced Raman scattering. (E) During the LbL process, Au precursors and reducing agent (NH2OH) were additionally added to accelerate increase of the layer thickness where Au precursors were reduced by NH2OH and affluent amine groups of PEI to make shell morphology with suprastructure.

TEM image after completion of SP synthesis step: (A) Step I, MNP@PEI, (B) IR0-LBL1, (C) IR150-LBL1, and (D) spiky core-shell SPs of IR300-LBL1. Inset figure on A and D shows size distribution of MNPs and core-shell SPs.

(A) Color of the solution in each growth step. (B) UV–Vis absorbance of (i) prepared PEI covered MNPs (100nm), (ii) after attaching AuNPs on MNPs (Step I), (iii) after 3 steps of growth, and (iv) after 6 steps of growth (Step III). Curve (v) is absorbance of AuNPs (5nm). (C) Zeta potential in Step I and Step II. (D) FT-IR transmittance of (i), (ii), (iv). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM images of MNPs (A) and magnetoplasmonic SPs after attaching AuNP seeds on MNPs (B), after 3 steps of growth (C), and 6 steps of growth (D).

Near-field enhancement within a model nanochain. (A) Near-field intensity distribution for a plane containing the axis of the chain, showing the electric field magnitude plotted in logarithmic color scale for illumination at 532nm of incident light parallel to the orientation of the chain and (B) electric field distribution for two types of geometry within gap of 1nm of Au shell in a nanochain. (C) Cross sectional illustration of the concave surface geometry of gold shell after growth step, and (D) electric field distribution of corresponded concave surface of gold shell. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SERS effect of concave MNP@Au SPs. (A) Raman signal of rhodamine B with and without concave SPs. (B) AFM image of Raman substrate made with discrete concave SPs. (C) Raman signal of GQDs (i) without concave SPs, (ii) with discrete concave SPs, (iii) with assembled concave SPs. (D) AFM image of Raman substrate made with assembled SP.

head.ce:title: Preparation of concave magnetoplasmonic core-shell supraparticles of gold-coated iron oxide via ion-reducible layer-by-layer method for surface enhanced Raman scattering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cogno-Mechatronics Engineering, Pusan National University, Busan 609-735, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Science and Technology, Shizuoka University, 836 Ohya Suruga-ku, Shizuoka 422-8529, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of suprastructure assemblies with unique colloidal and optical properties remains challenging. Non-uniform covering of magnetic nanoparticles (NPs) with an external inert Au shell has been attempted to protect the magnetic core against oxidation as well as to produce multifunctional supraparticles (SPs) possessing respective optical and magnetic properties. In this study, a concave Au NP coating was deposited on magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) with precise control of the shell thickness and roughness through a layer-by-layer (LbL) assisted ionic reduction method termed ion-reducible LbL (IR-LbL) method. Surface enhanced Raman spectra were obtained using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the magnetically aligned structure of the prepared core-shell SPs. It is probable that this synthesis method and the generated SPs are essential for characterizing the merge of electronics and magnetism in the nano-regime and may be applicable for further electronics, magnetic storage, and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Unique hybrid nanoscale suprastructures possessing dual magnetic and optical properties with colloidal nature are attractive owing to their potential applications, such as catalyst, surface enhanced Raman scattering, bio imaging and therapy [1–6]. In particular, concave metallic shell structures on a magnetic core are challenging to prepare with confinement of their unique opto-electromagnetic, catalytic, and biological properties originating from the abnormality of the electrical field in complicated surfaces [7–9]. These core-shell magnetoplasmonic supraparticles (SPs) can be functionalized with proteins, DNA, and other bio-molecules in combination with well-established gold-thiol surface chemistry for applications in bio-labeling, magnetic separation, and optical sensing [9]. Furthermore, these SPs have been extensively explored in pursuit of highly dense information storage devices and novel electronic components for spintronics [10,11]. For example, magnetic NPs with Au shell have been applied in external magnet-induced electronic circuits [12,13].

Many attempts have been made to manipulate the irregular morphology of the surface Au layer on the magnetic core to achieve non-uniformity. One representative method involves developing a limited passivation layer or epitaxial growth by reduction of Au ions on the surface of magnetic cores [14–18], in which, fine-tuning the size and shape of nano shells is crucial because of relationships between its topology and plasmonic property [19,20]. Recently, some groups presented novel approaches for achieving unique surface morphologies by anisotropic growth of Au layers [21,22]. Gold-coated iron core-shell and noble metal-coated magnetite NPs have also been synthesized using a reverse micelle method [23,24]. A polymer gap between the core and the shell was formulated to produce near infrared (NIR)-responsive Fe3O4@Au nanocomposites [25]. While these prior studies have demonstrated the viability of synthesizing and assembling core-shell type NPs, precise control of the continuity of the metal coating, coating thickness, and size monodispersity, and thin film assembly of the NPs remain major challenges [26]. Furthermore, the high roughness obtained via irregular passivation of Au precursors on the magnetic core is another challenge. Preparing such particles with a uniform size/shape and core-shell morphology is hampered by significant obstacles, largely because of the lack of understanding of their formation mechanism and control of thermodynamic equivalence during the synthesis. In general, the concave structures are thermodynamically unstable and can potentially transform into spherical particles with time [27]. The formation of spiked or bristled particles is currently attributed to different tendencies of the stabilizers, as exemplified by thioglycolic acid [34], poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) [9], and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) [4], which are absorbed on diverse crystal faces [9,10]. In our previous paper, we reported a unique metallic core-shell nanocomplex formed by the self-assembly method, i.e., spiky gold-coated iron SPs (Fe3O4@Au SPs) with diameters of 105–185nm that exhibited strong magnetization [21]. These species exhibit unique physiochemical properties because of their well-defined shape and distinctive topological structure. However, the synthesis approach was still not attractive as a robust method for coating AuNPs in any core structure owing to the complex preparation process. We proposed gold-coated iron oxide core-shell supraparticles which has polyethylimine (PEI) as an anchoring group, since the PEI is effective connecting agent to make gold shells on the surface of inorganic nanostructures because it has rich amine groups which is adhesive to gold [28,29]. While the experimental process, a AuNPs coating was deposited on magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) with precise control of the shell thickness and roughness through a PEI mediated layer-by-layer (LbL) assisted ionic reduction method, termed ion-reducible LbL (IR-LbL). LbL itself is an excellent technique for controlling the thickness of a coating layer by layer a sequentially repeated process [30]. However, when it is applied to the control of shell thickness growth, loss of most of the NPs is inevitable because of complicated aggregation due to various charge-charge interactions. Thus, a novel process is suggested wherein a minimal LbL process is conducted by changing the concentration or binding time of the polymer, leading to remarkable control of the shell thickness in the range of 2–3nm in one round of the LbL process. After the process to forming the shell on MNPs unique morphology of Au shell which is composed of self-assembled AuNPs’ suprastructure anchored with PEI was observed by electron microscope, called supraparticle (SP). Highly enhanced Raman scattering signal of rhodamine-B and GQDs were measured on SPs’ surface and additional enhancement was obtained on magnetically aligned structure of SPs. It is probable that this synthesis method and the generated SPs are essential for characterizing the merging of electronics and magnetism in the nano-regime and may be applicable for further electronics, magnetic storage, and biomedical applications.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, branched, MW : ∼25,000gmol−1), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH⋅HCl), Iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3⋅6H2O), sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc), 8-mercaptooctanoic acid (8-MOA), ethylene glycol, poly(diallyldimethyl ammonium chloride) (PDDA), and poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Inc. (Milwaukee, WI). Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Yakuri Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Kyoto, Japan). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was obtained from Junsei Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chuo-ku, Japan). Trisodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7⋅3H2O) and cysteamine (NH2CH2CH2SH) were obtained from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland). Ethanol was obtained from SK Chemicals (Seongnam, South Korea). Deionized water (>18.2mcm−1) was used in most experiments (Human Corporation, Seoul, South Korea). All chemicals were used as received with no further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Step I: synthesis of PEI-coated MNPs (MNP@PEI)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, magnetic NPs were synthesized by solvothermal synthesis [31]. The surface of the prepared NPs was modified with PEI (denoted as MNP@PEI). In detail, 0.68g of FeCl3⋅6H2O was dissolved in 20ml of ethylene glycol to form a transparent solution. Subsequently, 1.2g of sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc) and 0.5g of PEI were added to the solution. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 20min at 60°C and transferred to a home-made autoclave and reacted at 220°C for 2h. The resultant black precipitates were rinsed three times with deionized water and ethanol to separate the supernatant with the assistance of a permanent magnet. The precipitates were then dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C under 0.05atm of pressure for 12h and redispersed in deionized water (2mgmL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Step II: AuNP decorated MNPs (MNP@PEI@Au SPs) by LbL method
ce:section.ce:para: The electrostatic interaction between positively charged PEI and the negatively charged AuNPs was exploited to synthesize the MNP@PEI@Au SPs via the facile LbL method. First, colloidal AuNPs (∼3nm) were produced in separate glassware by reducing HAuCl4 (1w/v%) using NaBH4 (0.075w/v%) in sodium citrate (38.8mmolL−1) [32]. The aqueous solution of the prepared MNP@PEI (1mgmL−1) was then added to 5mL of the prepared AuNP solution, where the electrostatic surface charge of each NP was +34.2mV and −22.4mV, respectively. The mixed solution was sonicated for 1min and this solution was gently shaken for 2h until the color changed from brown to reddish brown. Finally, the MNP@PEI@AuNP composite was separated from the solution using a permanent magnet and washed thrice with deionized water to remove any unbound AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Step III: precise control of the shell thickness by IR-LbL process
ce:section.ce:para:
The thickness of the Au shell of the MNP@PEI@AuNP was precisely controlled by adding Au precursors. First, the MNP@PEI@AuNP composite was diluted in a 5mgmL−1 PEI solution at 60°C for 2h to develop a second layer, and the products were treated by following the separation and cleaning process described in Section 2.2. The final product was dispersed in 5mL deionized water. The gold shell was grown by the IR-LbL process using the designated amount of Au precursor. In detail, 2mL of the prepared MNP@PEI@AuNP@PEI was sonicated with 11mL of 0.01M NaOH (pH 11.5) in a Branson 8510 sonication bath (320W, 40kHz, Branson, Danbury, CT). In the first growth step, 50μL of 1w/v% HAuCl4 was added along with 75µL of 0.2M NH2OH⋅HCl. From the second step to the last, 25μL of 0.2M NH2OH⋅HCl was added after the injection of 50μL of 1w/v% HAuCl4 solution. The mixture was sonicated for 10min between each growth step, where reduction of the Au ions proceeded as illustrated in Fig. 1 E. Eq. (1) represents the chemical reaction for reduction of Au3+ to Au0 by NH2OH in PEI rich surroundings during the IR-LbL process [33].
(1)
PEI
(
NH
3 +
)
n
+
xHAuCl
4
→ PEI
(
NH
3 +
)
n - x
+ PEI
(
NO
2
)
x
+
Au
0

Indeed, the affluent amine groups of PEI, represented as PEI(NH3 +)n, lead to Au reduction on the surface of MNP by partial self-oxidation to PEI(NO2)x, where x represents the amount of oxidized amine groups of PEI, which is directly correlated to the amount of reduced Au ions. This IR-LbL process was repeated to obtain the designated gold shell. The synthesized MNP@(PEI@AuNP) n composites, where n is the repetition number, were separated from the reaction mixture and rinsed. Note that in the present experiment, the minimal number of n was intentionally applied in order to avoid any uncontrollable aggregation. When the LbL process was repeated once, the initial concentration of the Au precursors (1w/v%) was changed to 0–300μL, where the sample code IR150 indicates that 150μL of the Au precursor was added in the LbL process. UV–Vis spectrophotometric and electron microscopic measurements were carried out to observe the change in the shell thickness of the prepared core-shell nanomaterials.

The size and shape of the products were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM H-7600, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Scanning electron microscope images were acquired with a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM S-4700, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA) was used to obtain absorption spectra of the prepared SPs. The surface charge of the SPs was measured with a zeta-sizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectroscopy of 1 – dimensionally aligned MNP@Au SPs
ce:section.ce:para: SERS were obtained by using GQDs on the magnetically aligned structure of the prepared SPs. To prepare a SERS-inducing layer, a silicon wafer was coated with PDDA and PAA by the typical LbL method. The SPs were aligned on the (PDDA/PAA)1-coated silica substrate by using an external magnet (neodymium N50, 40×20×5mm, 2400 G). A drop of the cysteamine-functionalized NP solution was coated on the surface of the PDDA/PAA-coated silica and an external magnetic field of 5 Oe was applied until the sample was totally dried at 25°C, usually for 12h. A 30μL aliquot of GQDs (1mgml−1) was deposited and washed gently using deionized water. The details of GQD preparation and characterization can be found in the Supporting Information (SI). The Raman signal was monitored using a Ramboss micro-Raman measurement system (Dongwoo Optron Inc., Gwangju, South Korea) where the spot size of the laser light is 1μm.

We used the COMSOL Multiphysics to verify the local electric fields distribution of core-shell NPs and nanochains with three dimensional models. The refractive indexes of iron oxide and air are set as 2.42 and 1, respectively and that of gold was obtained from the Johnson and Christy values [34].

The morphological variances of the synthesized materials were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). The synthesized PEI-coated iron oxide mostly had a spherical morphology and the size distributions of the PEI-coated iron oxide were centered at 108.39 and 10.51nm from the electron microscopic measurements (Fig. 2A), where the opaque layers around the SPs in the TEM image in Fig. 2A indicate PEI polymers in which the emitted electron beam was scattered due to its dielectric constant and refractive index. A seed growth method was applied to produce gold shells on the MNP@PEI (Step II). First, negatively charged gold seeds with a size of 4nm were attached to MNP@PEI via electrostatic and weak covalent bonding with amino groups. Fig. 2B shows the TEM image after completing Step II of the synthesis process, i.e., formation of Au-NP decorated MNPs by the LbL method (MNP@PEI@AuNP), where a small amount of the 4nm AuNPs was attached to the MNPs. It is probable that the size of the PEI layer decreased during the several washing processes in Step II. However, Fig. 2C shows clear formation of Au NPs on the surface of MNP after 3 consecutive ion reducing processes (Step III), that is, SP formation of MNP(@PEI@AuNP)1, and most of the surfaces were coated with Au NPs after 6 steps of the ion reducing process (Fig. 2D). After 6 repetitions of the ion reducing process, the size of the core-shell SPs increased to 122.53±21.33nm. A Au shell with roughly 14.19nm thickness was established after just 6 repetitions of the ion reducing step after the LbL step. Interestingly, a unique core-shell morphology was synthesized during the shell growth process; i.e., epitaxial growth of Au nanorods was observed in all directions on the surface of the Au shell (Fig. 2D). This is comparable to other LBL data where the shell growth was a few nm after several repeating processes [35,36].

Fig. 3 A presents photographic images of the prepared solutions showing the color change depending on the synthesis step, and Fig. 3B shows the corresponding UV–Vis spectra. The samples are denoted as (i) MNP@PEI, (ii) IR000-LBL1, (iii) IR150-LBL1, and (iv) IR300-LBL1. Sample (i) was dark brown, (ii) had a brighter color and its UV–Vis spectrum had a broader peak because of binding of the AuNPs on MNP@PEI, sample (iii) was greenish blue, and (iv) was dark purple. In the UV–Vis spectra, a unique peak near 700nm was generated when additional HAuCl4 and NH2OH were added to the prepared MNP@PEI@AuNP colloidal solution (i.e., during the IR-LbL synthesis process, Step III), resulting in growth of the AuNPs on MNP@PEI to a larger size with epitaxial growth of many 1D rods. After 6 repetitions of the ion reducing process, the Au shell was fully covered with concave surfaces of many spikes, as observed in the TEM image (Fig. 2D). The UV–Vis absorbance peak also became broadened in the wavelength range of 500–1100nm, which corresponds to the conjugation of longitudinal mode of the plasmonic absorption in 1D metallic nanomaterials [37]. The surface charge of the MNP@PEI@Au SPs was measured (Fig. 3C). MNPs coated with positively charged polymer and AuNPs covered with negatively charged surfactant were sequentially deposited on the surface of the MNPs. During the LbL process, the surface charge in the range of −10 to +40mV corresponded to the external layers of PEI or AuNP, leading to control of the shell thickness and roughness under stable SP conditions. The surface charge of the MNP@PEI and AuNPs was +34.2mV and −22.4mV, respectively. After Step I, the zeta potential of the satellite structure was lower than that of the AuNP itself. The outer layer of PEI was not fully covered with AuNPs, thus the exposed PEI decreased the surface charge. After Step II, the nanostructure of the nanocomposite had a slightly more positive charge compared with the original surface charge of the PEI-coated MNPs. Previous reports show that when positive and negative materials are repeatedly deposited on NPs by the LbL process, the zeta potential of the NPs increases and reaches saturation when this value reaches the limit [38]. Fig. 3D presents the FTIR spectroscopic data for the prepared SPs in each step of the synthesis. The FT-IR spectrum of PEI shows two bands at 3350 and 3285cm−1 that arise from NH2 stretching vibrations, strong peaks at 2938 and 2823cm−1 that can be assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of the CH2 group, respectively, and a peak at 1459cm−1 that corresponds to in-plane bending of the CH2 group. The peaks for the bending vibration of the NH group and the stretching vibration of the CN groups of PEI were observed at 1598cm−1 and 1120cm−1, respectively. During growth of the Au shell on MNP@PEI, the intensity of the peaks around 3000cm−1 and 1500cm−1 increased and the peaks became sharper. It is plausible that the physicochemical environment near PEI is changed due to the large amount of free electrons of Au during shell growth.

A unique IR-LbL assembly method was utilized for the formation of concave SPs. Note that these structures are not thermodynamically stable in the synthesis and it is usual to become spherical shape without further blocking treatment. In our experiment, this was accomplished by exploiting NP self-assembly around amino groups on PEI that is a key chemical to induce a concave nanostructure by reducing extra Au ions and blocking parts of Au plates on MNP to avoid further homogeneous lattice growth. Negatively-charged AuNPs were attached to the positively charged surface of MNP covered with PEI. A second layer of PEI was again deposited on this composite. Amino-rich surroundings around the Au islands were formed with this second layer. When additional reduced Au0 atoms were bound on the negatively-charged Au NPs surface, the Au islands on the MNP became larger (see Fig. 1E for schematic understanding). When the size of the Au islands increased, further attachment of Au0 atoms on the AuNPs became energetically unfavorable. Further addition of HAuCl4 and NH2OH resulted in formation of more Au0 atoms. Au0 atoms were attracted to the amino-rich sites of PEI around the gold islands rather than on the surface of the Au islands. By controlling the number of gold islands on the MNPs and the amount of PEI covering the composite, the size and shape of the magneto-plasmonic nanocomposite can be marginally controlled (Fig. 4 ).

Interestingly, in our experiment, the composite had a unique surface morphology derived from the second layer of PEI. In the first stage when HAuCl4 and NH2OH are added to the MNP@PEI@AuNP colloidal solution, reduced Au0 preferentially bind with Au atoms on the surface of the AuNPs, which then bind with PEI, and the size of the AuNPs increases. When more precursors are added to the gold, Au0 atoms around this composite tend to attach to the amines on PEI more than to the AuNPs on the MNPs. According to the placement of the amines on PEI based on its carbon structure, the Au0 atoms form Au-shells with different surface morphologies, generating a rough surface. Moreover, the morphology of the gold around the MNPs depends on the carbon structure of PEI. PEI geometrically blocks the growth direction of gold on the surface of MNP, leading to a bumpy gold surface as shown in the SEM images of Fig. 4C and D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational and experimental data of SERS on 1D-assembled concave MNP@Au SPs
ce:section.ce:para: A nanochain model was simulated for the electric field intensity and distribution at the excitation wavelength of 532nm (Fig. 5 A). The nanochain model consists of 7 NPs with a diameter of 100nm of iron oxide core and 14nm thickness of Au shell in the absence of concave surface. It is shown that the electric fields are strongly confined in the gaps between gold shells and shows plasmon coupling effect excited by polarized light parallel to the axis of the nanochain. For more detail approach, two different identical geometric models (parallel or zigzag aligned) were designed for plasmon coupling occurred within the gaps of the concave gold shells in the nanochain (Fig. 5B). Both show strong interaction from unique morphology of the surface. In the parallel aligned structure, more signal enhancement was observed than the zigzag aligned morphology. But both expected 104 times higher signal enhancement than single NP from this simulation. Fig. 5C depicts the schematic illustration of the concave surface of gold shell after the growth step. The drastic electric field is observed on the center of gaps (1nm) between concave surfaces and the maximum SERS enhancement factor (EF) was obtained from the largest local field intensity in the form of |E532|4/|E0|4 [39], where E532 is electric field intensity at wavelength of 532nm and E0 is that of incident electric field. Notably, the EF on the order of over 103 is increased compared with single concave surface in Fig. 5D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS effect on rhodamine B and GQDs on magnetically self-assembled concave MNP@Au SPs
ce:section.ce:para: The well-known Raman spectral characteristics of GQDs are the G band (∼1580cm−1), which is generally assigned to the E2g phonon of sp2 bonds of carbon atoms, and the D band (∼1350cm−1) as a breathing mode of k-point phonons with A1g symmetry [40–42]. Fig. 6 A presents Raman signals of rhodamine B with and without the concave magnetoplasmonic SPs in which high Raman signal enhancement was observed. Fig. 6B presents its AFM image. Fig. 6C shows the Raman spectra of GQDs on different substrates. The spectra demonstrate that the concave SPs enhanced the Raman signal, where the non-Platonic concave suprastructure with many AuNPs induced unique optical properties and can be used as substrates for SERS since there are extraordinary hotspots around the rough surface of the gold shell [43–45]. Indeed, the Raman signals of the GQDs on discrete concave magnetoplasmonic SPs were about 3–4 times stronger than that of the GQDs on discrete SPs. Furthermore, it was more interesting that there was geometric correspondence of signal enhancement. When the SPs were deposited on the designated area with random and 1-dimensional orientation, the efficiency to enhance the Raman signal of GQDs was swiftly different. When MNP@Au SPs were assembled by using an external magnetic field 3–4 times greater signal enhancement was observed. It is thoughtfully comprehensible that this is because more amounts of NPs are resided in the given area exposed to the laser beam when MNP@Au SPs were assembled and eventually, more hotspots can be generated in denser area. The results suggest that the dominant enhancement mechanism is due to a very intense local electromagnetic field that is created by plasmon coupling of self-assembled concave Au shells at the junction of the MNP@Au SPs and the alignment of SPs can induce 3–4-fold higher efficiency of signal enhancement [46].

Concave MNP@Au SPs were synthesized through a facile layer-by-layer (LbL) assisted ionic reduction method with precise control of shell thickness and roughness. This method offers the following distinctive features: (i) In previous reports about MNP@Au core-shell nanoparticle, most shell structures are composed of Au ions, resulting in smooth surface crystalline shell was constructed. However, in proposed method, we synthesized the shell via self-assembly process from many small sizes of AuNP seeds by repeated ion-reduction process, resulting in very rough (concave) surfaces were formed. (ii) The connecting agent, polyethylimine (PEI), possess a rich amine groups, which provides an effective anchoring layer and assists the reduction of the Au ions during the repeated growth steps. The resulting structure of core-shell type supraparticles with highly rough Au shell structure was observed by TEM and SEM. And the SERS data indicate 3–4 folds greater enhancement of the Raman signal for MNP@Au SPs under an external magnetic field. With the 1 dimensional alignment of the SPs, another 3–4 folds higher enhancement was observed. Signal enhancement mechanism is mainly due to a highly intense local electromagnetic field created by plasmon coupling of self-assembled concave gold nanospheres at the junction of the SP assembly, which is well correlated with computational simulation. Because of its novel structure with precise controlling method the present synthesis methods and the generated suprastructure are essential for characterizing the merge of electronics and magnetism in the nano-regime and are applicable for further electronics, magnetic storage, and biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Korean Health Technology R&D Project, Ministry of Health & Welfare, Republic of Korea (HI16C1553), Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (NRF-2016R1A2B4012072) and supported by the Commercializations Promotion Agency for R&D Outcomes (COMPA) funded by the Korea Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (MSIP) (2016K000135).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.03.098.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Core/shell nanoparticles: classes, properties, synthesis mechanisms, characterization, and applications
Colloidal core–shell materials with ‘spiky’surfaces assembled from gold nanorods
